coredata.dc:title: Adsorption and detection of heavy metals from aqueous water by PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Zhao, Haiyang
Zhang, Dongdong
Sun, Heyu
Zhao, Yiping
Xie, Manman
coredata.dc:description: The irreversibility, long-term and toxicity variability of heavy metal ion pollution make it necessary to eliminate it through reasonable detection methods and control methods. carbon dots (CDs) are an efficient fluorescent probe widely used in the detection of heavy metal ions, but the difficulty of recycling nanomaterials limits its practicability. Herein, by reacting CDs with attapulgite (ATP) in the PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane, we successfully prepared a PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane with dual functions of adsorption and detection of heavy metal ions. Due to the super-hydrophilic nature of CDs, PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane exhibit significantly high wettability (contact angle reduced to 0 ° within 6 s) and permeability (110 L/m2h), which is conducive to its application in water treatment. The addition of ATP significantly improves the adsorption capacity of the composite membrane for Hg2+ (0.99 mg/g), Cu2+ (0.73 mg/g) and Fe3+ (0.60 mg/g), which also overcomes the difficulty of ATP recovery and secondary pollution problems. After being adsorbed, the heavy metal ions are complexed with the CDs on the surface of the composite membrane, quenching the fluorescence of the CDs for detection of heavy metal ions. With its high-efficiency ion adsorption and detection capabilities, PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membranes, which have the advantages of fast, stable, environmentally friendly, simple and inexpensive, will become a promising heavy metal ion detection and adsorption material.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
ATP
PVDF membrane
Adsorption
Heavy metals
Detection

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was financially sponsored by The Science and Technology Plans of Tianjin ( 17PTSYJC00040 and 18PTSYJC00180 ), Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Ministry of Education of China (No. IRT-17R80 ), Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (No. TD13-5044 ) and Tianjin Postgraduate Research and Innovation Project (No. 2019YJSB195 ). We would gratefully acknowledge the help of the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University.

This work was financially sponsored by The Science and Technology Plans of Tianjin (17PTSYJC00040 and 18PTSYJC00180), Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Ministry of Education of China (No. IRT-17R80), Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (No. TD13-5044) and Tianjin Postgraduate Research and Innovation Project (No. 2019YJSB195). We would gratefully acknowledge the help of the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the synthesis for CDs.
Schematic illustration of modified CDs by KH-560.
Schematic diagram of preparation of PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane.
(a) FT-IR spectrum, (b) UV–vis absorption and (c) PL emission spectra (λex=380 nm) of CDs and CDs-KH560. The inset image is the photographs of CDs and CDs-KH560 under Day light and 365 nm UV light.
(a) FT-IR spectra and (b) XPS survey spectra of PVDF, PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane and the PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane; (c) TG curves of ATP, PVDF membrane and PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane.

SEM image of membranes: (a)-(c) top surface, (a1) - (c1) cross-sectional and (a2) - (c2) high magnification cross-sectional morphology of PVDF, PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane and the PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane respectively. ATP (orange dotted circles) and CDs (yellow dashed circles) were observed in the surface and cross-sectional structure of PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane and the PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane.
(a) XRD patterns, (b) Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and (c) Raman spectrum PVDF, PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane and the PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane respectively.
Surface AFM image of pristine PVDF membrane, PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane and PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane.
(a) Water contact angle, (b) pure water flux and (c) Cu2+, Fe3+ and Hg2+ adsorption of pristine PVDF membrane, PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane and PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane.
Fluorescence spectrum of PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane after a different time in water (Photographs of different membranes under Day light and 365 nm UV light in the set).
Fluorescence quenching diagram of Cu2+, Fe3+ and Hg2+ to PVDF, PVDF/ATP and PVDF/ATP-CDs membranes. The inset image is the enlarged picture of fluorescence quenching spectra.
Pictorial representation showed the fluorescence detection and adsorption mechanism of PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane for heavy metal ions.
Total pore volume and average pore diameter of pure PVDF, PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane and PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane measured by BTE method.

head.ce:title: Adsorption and detection of heavy metals from aqueous water by PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Zhangjiakou Cigarette Factory Co., ltd, Zhangjiakou 075000, Hebei, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Zhangjiakou Cigarette Factory Co., ltd, Zhangjiakou 075000, Hebei, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Separation Membranes and Membrane Processes, School of Materials Science and Engineering, Tiangong University, Tianjin 300387, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Institute of Seawater Desalination and Multipurpose Utilization, MNR(Tianjin)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: The Institute of Seawater Desalination and Multipurpose Utilization, MNR(Tianjin), Tianjin, 300192, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Radiology and Imaging Sciences, Emory University, Atlanta, GA, USA

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Radiology and Imaging Sciences, Emory University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Radiology and Imaging Sciences, Emory University, Atlanta, Georgia, USA

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The irreversibility, long-term and toxicity variability of heavy metal ion pollution make it necessary to eliminate it through reasonable detection methods and control methods. carbon dots (CDs) are an efficient fluorescent probe widely used in the detection of heavy metal ions, but the difficulty of recycling nanomaterials limits its practicability. Herein, by reacting CDs with attapulgite (ATP) in the PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane, we successfully prepared a PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane with dual functions of adsorption and detection of heavy metal ions. Due to the super-hydrophilic nature of CDs, PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane exhibit significantly high wettability (contact angle reduced to 0 ° within 6 s) and permeability (110 L/m2h), which is conducive to its application in water treatment. The addition of ATP significantly improves the adsorption capacity of the composite membrane for Hg2+ (0.99 mg/g), Cu2+ (0.73 mg/g) and Fe3+ (0.60 mg/g), which also overcomes the difficulty of ATP recovery and secondary pollution problems. After being adsorbed, the heavy metal ions are complexed with the CDs on the surface of the composite membrane, quenching the fluorescence of the CDs for detection of heavy metal ions. With its high-efficiency ion adsorption and detection capabilities, PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membranes, which have the advantages of fast, stable, environmentally friendly, simple and inexpensive, will become a promising heavy metal ion detection and adsorption material.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, a large number of pollutants from agricultural runoff, industrial wastewater, sewage treatment plants and other human activities will eventually enter the marine environment with the rapid development of industry and social economy [1]. Remarkably, heavy metals that enter the water system or food system in different forms are usually non-biodegradable, leading to accumulation in their organisms. In particular, the toxic metal ions mercury (Hg2+), iron (Fe3+) and copper (Cu2+) can cause poisoning problems and endanger the body's metabolic process [2,3]. In this regard, it is urgent to develop an effective method for detecting heavy metals in wastewater, which is essential to eliminate metal pollution in wastewater. Up to now, a variety of technical methods have been developed to detect harmful metal ions in wastewater systems, such as atomic absorption spectroscopy, inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry, atomic absorption spectroscopy, and electrochemical detection [4,5]. Remarkably, fluorescence spectroscopy method has attracted more and more attention because of its simplicity, rapidity and sensitivity [6–8]. More importantly, this method can achieve trace detection of heavy metal ions [9]. Thus, it can be said that the use of fluorescence spectroscopy to detect heavy metal ions in wastewater systems has a good development prospect.

As a new fluorescent nano-carbon material, fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have received widespread attention due to their low toxicity, good biocompatibility, good fluorescence stability, adjustable excitation and emission wavelengths, high fluorescence intensity, and outstanding electrical properties [10,11]. At present, most scholars believe that the luminescence of CDs is largely related to the carbon-core or surface domain, and the recombination of electron holes in the carbon-core and the electron migration in the surface domain will all lead to the generation of fluorescence [12,13]. When CDs are in contact with heavy metal ions, their fluorescence is easily quenched by electron acceptors [14]. At present, it is generally believed that the mechanism by which the fluorescence of CDs is quenched by heavy metal ions is: the interaction between heavy metal ions and CDs changes the exciton distribution of CDs and transfers their electrons, charges or energy, thereby promoting the extinction of excitons' radiation less electron/hole recombination [15,16]. In addition, another more recognized statement is that the absorption spectrum of the newly formed substance from the interaction of heavy metal ions with CDs overlaps with the absorption spectrum or emission spectrum of the CDs itself, and then absorbs excitation or emission light to cause fluorescence quenching [17]. Therefore, CDs can be used as an efficient fluorescent probe to be widely used in the detection of heavy metal ions [18]. Remarkably, it is worth noting that powdered CDs with extremely strong hydrophilicity require a carrier to fix them so that they can be more conveniently applied to the detection of heavy metal ions in different environments [19].

As a one-dimensional nanoscale hydrated aluminum magnesium silicate clay mineral, attapulgite (ATP) is widely used for the adsorption and removal of pollutants due to its high specific surface area, moderate cation exchange capacity (CEC) and excellent chemical stability [20]. The ATP/C nanocomposite prepared by Wang et al. [4] has high adsorption capacity for Cu2+, Pb2+ and Cd2+ in a wide pH range, and can quickly reach the adsorption equilibrium. More importantly, the adsorbed ATP/C material still has good resorption performance after acid washing. However, ATP is difficult to separate from the solution after adsorbing metal ions, leading to secondary pollution. Recently, Poly(vinylidene fluoride) (PVDF) was employed as a support or substrate to prepare composite membranes due to its chemical and mechanical stability, and environmentally friendly behavior [21,22]. According to reports in the literature, nanoparticles as an excellent additive for hydrophilic modification can improve the membrane structure, making the hybrid membrane has both high temperature resistance and good toughness [23]. Additionally, ATP doped PVDF were reported to exhibit enhanced adsorption and removal performance for metal ions [24,25]. As reported by Xing et al., the combination of ATP nanoparticles and PVDF polymer chains increases the energy required to decompose the PVDF molecular chains, which in turn leads to an increase in the thermal stability of the hybrid membrane [26]. From the perspective of thermodynamic interaction, this miscible system, which is observed in the absence of specific interactions, can be explained by the mean field theory [27].

Hence, the combination of PVDF, ATP and CDs can effectively separate the adsorption material from the liquid phase. In earlier study [28],we developed PVDF-g-PAA-CDs membrane with stable fluorescence by grafting carbon dots (CDs) onto the surface of PVDF-g-PAA membrane, and we found that the addition of CDs gives the composite membrane a significant fluorescence quenching effect on Cu2+, Hg2+ and Fe3+. In this contribution, we successfully prepared PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane with dual functions of adsorption and detection of heavy metal ions by reacting the hybrid membrane made of PVDF and ATP with CDs-KH560. The morphology of PVDF/ATP-CDs membrane was observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM) and atomic force microscopy (AFM). The thermogravimetric analysis (TGA) was used to evaluate the content of ATP in the PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane. The water contact angle was used to detect the hydrophilicity of the membrane surface. The photoluminescence performance can be used to detect metal ions, according to the quenching phenomenon. The adsorption capacity of the PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane was also evaluated.

Citric acid, ethylenediamine, Fe2(SO4)3‧xH2O, CuSO4‧5 H2O and KH-560 were supplied by heowns. Polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) was purchased form Solvay Korea Co., ATP was purchased from Jiangsu Xuyi Xinyuan Technology Co., Ltd. N, N-dimethylformamide (DMF) and PEG10000 were purchased from Tianjin Kemiou Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd.

CDs were prepared as follows [29]: ethylenediamine (536 µL) and citric acid (0.46 g) was dissolved in pure-water (10 mL). After being transferred to a poly (tetrafluoroethylene) autoclave (20 mL), the mixed solution was heated at 200 ºC for 5 h. Hereafter, the reactors were cooled to room temperature naturally with the product brown-black and transparent. The preparation diagram is shown in Fig. 1.

CDs-KH560 were synthesized with the following method. Firstly, 0.05 g of the synthesized CDs was dissolved in 20 mL of deionized water with sonicate for 20 min. Then, 2 mL of KH-560 was slowly dropped the mixture with rapid stirring at 30 ℃ for 24 h to obtain KH-560 modified CDs solution (CDs-KH560). The reaction diagram is shown in Fig. 2:

Using an immersion phase inversion process, 16 g of polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF) powder, 8 g of PEG 10,000, and 0.8 g of ATP are added to 76 g of DMF solution under stirring. The mixed system was stirred at 60 °C for 5 h to obtain a uniform casting solution. Then, the resulting solution was placed in a drying oven for 2 h to remove air bubbles. After casting the casting solution on the glass plate with a casting knife (300 µm), the glass plate was immediately immersed in a water coagulation bath at 25 °C. Finally, the obtained wet PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane was washed with water and stored in distilled water until use.

To prepare PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membranes, the PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane was cut it into several uniform shapes and immersed in an aqueous solution containing a certain mass of CDs-KH560, and shacked at a constant speed in a shaker at 60 ℃ to react for 24 h. After the reaction completed, the membranes were taken out and washed several times with a large amount of deionized water. The preparation process of PVDF/ATP-CDs membrane was shown in Fig. 3. As a control, a pure PVDF membrane with a uniform shape was immersed in the CDs-KH560 solution and prepared with the same steps.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Tensor37, Bruker, Germany) was used to detect chemical compositions of the CDs, CDs-KH560 and surface of membranes recorded in the wave number range of 4000–500 cm−1. The surface elemental composition was detected by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Genesis 60 S, EDAX Inc., Mahwah, NJ, USA). Thermogravimetric analysis (TG) analysis was performed with a STA409PC TG analyzer (NETZSCH, Germany). UV–visible was done using a UV–vis spectrophotometer (TU-1901, Beijing, China) to evaluate the absorbance of the CDs and CDs-KH560. The cross-section and top surface morphologies of the membranes were identified by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, s-4800, Japan). The hydrophilicity of the membranes was recorded by measuring contact angle of the pure water on the membrane top surface with a contact angle instrument (DSA100, Krüss, Germany). The surface topography of the samples was measured using an atomic force microscopy (AFM) (Agilent 5500, Icon, USA). The fluorescence intensity of the samples was measured by fluorospectrophotometer (F-380, Gangdong Sci&Tech. Development, Tianjin).

The filtration experiment was carried out with our self-made equipment. The membrane sample (5 cm) was fixed in the filter cell and pressurized at 0.2 MPa for 0.5 h. After the pressure was reduced to 0.1 MPa, the water flux of the membrane was measured. The pure water flux (Jw) was calculated by the following equation[30]:
(2−1)
J
w
=
V
A × ∆ t
Where V is the volume of permeated water (L), A is the membrane area (m2), and Δt is the permeation time (h).

The adsorption properties of samples were investigated with ICPAES (Model: Varian 715-ES, US). After being lyophilized, the membrane was cut into 0.5 × 0.5 cm2 and placed in 6 mL of 20 mg/L Cu2+ metal solution. The concentration of tested metal ions in the solution before and after tested was recorded by ICP and calculated as follows [31].
(2−2)
Q =
C
0
−
C
f
× V
m
Where C
0 and C
f (mg/L) are the actual concentrations of metal ions in the solution before and after adsorption by the hydrogels samples; V (L) is the volume of the metal ions solution; m (g) is the weight of the original membrane used to test.

In order to explore the surface properties of synthetic CDs, we used FT-IR spectroscopy to characterize the synthetic materials [32]. As shown in Fig. 4(a), the spectrum of CDs shows characteristic peaks of -OH, -NH2, -CO-NH-, and -COOH [33]. The existence of these hydrophilic groups is caused by the good hydrophilic properties of CDs. Compared with the spectrum of CDs, the spectrum of CDs-K560 has Si-O and C-O-Si absorption peaks at 1030 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1, respectively, indicating the successful grafting of KH-560 in CDs. Compared with CDs, the spectrum of CDs-K560 has Si-O and C-O-Si absorption peaks at 1030 cm−1 and 1120 cm−1, indicating the successful grafting of KH-560 in CDs. In addition, the UV-Vis absorption spectra of CDs and CDs-KH560 both show the new absorption peaks near 344 nm and 240 nm (Fig. 4(b)), which belong to the strong absorption peaks of the surface state or molecular state and the carbon nucleus, respectively [34]. After the reaction with KH-560, the content and distribution of -NH2 on the surface of CDs changed, resulting in a slight decrease in the absorbance of its absorption peak at 344 nm. Remarkably, the fluorescence emitted by CDs under the excitation of ultraviolet (UV) lamps is beneficial to people's visual inspection [33]. Additionally, it can be seen from the PL emission spectra (λex=380 nm) of CDs and CDs-KH560 in Fig. 4(c) that CDs and CDs-KH560 exhibit strong blue fluorescence at 450 nm after being excited. Compared with CDs, the fluorescence intensity of CDs-KH560 has decreased, which is related to the luminescence mechanism of CDs. Speciﬁcally, the luminescence mechanism of surface and edge effects is: there are a variety of functional groups on the surface or edge of the structure of CDs, and the fluorescence characteristics caused by the influence of surface functional groups or edge defects [35,36]. After reacting with KH-560, the surface functional group content of CDs changes, resulting in a decrease in the fluorescence intensity of CDs-KH560. From the photos of CDs and CDs-KH560 under daylight and 365 nm ultraviolet light (the inset in Fig. 4(c)), it can be seen that both are both brown-yellow under sunlight, but emit strong blue fluorescence under 365 nm ultraviolet light, further indicating that the prepared CDs-KH560 still maintains the fluorescence characteristics of CDs.

Generally, FT-IR spectroscopy provides information about the constituents in the surface of polymeric matrix and modifiers [37]. As shown in Fig. 5(a), there are three characteristic absorption peaks at wavelengths of 1409 cm−1, 1182 cm−1 and 1068 cm−1 in the spectra of pure PVDF membrane, corresponding to the asymmetric vibrational absorption peaks of methylene C-H connected to C-F2 and C-F [38]. In contrast, the spectra of PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane and PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane show two new characteristic absorption peaks at wavelengths of 1030 cm−1 and 985 cm−1, which belong to the stretching of Si-O and Si-O-Si, conforming the presence of ATP in the hybrid membrane [39]. Compared with PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane, a new peak appears on the PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane at the wave location of 1112 cm−1, which was attributed to the C-O-Si of ATP-CDs, indicating that CDs successfully reacted with ATP on PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane.

The measurement of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was used to further determine the surface chemical compositions of the pure PVDF, PVDF/ATP and PVDF/ATP-CDs membranes and the results are shown in Fig. 5(b). It can be seen that the full spectrum of the pure PVDF membrane showed obvious F1s, O1s, and C1s characteristic signals at 942.1 eV, 530.5 eV, and 284.1 eV, while the PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane showed Si characteristic signals at 101.4 eV [40]. Since ATP is a silicate mineral rich in magnesium and aluminum, the appearance of the Si characteristic signal indicates that ATP has been successfully blended into the PVDF membrane. From the pattern of PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane, an obvious N characteristic signal can be seen at 400.3 eV, which is the reason why CDs are rich in nitrogen. In addition, compared with the PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane, the Si content in the PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane is significantly increased, which is caused by the inclusion of silicon in the KH-560 modified CDs. Consistent with the infrared results in Fig. 5(a), the XPS results also show that CDs were successfully grafted to the surface of the PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane, making sufficient preparations for the adsorption and detection of heavy metal ions.

To explore the actual blending amount of ATP in PVDF membrane, TG analysis was performed on ATP, PVDF and PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane. [41] As shown in Fig. 5(c), PVDF membrane begins to decompose at 375.1 ℃, while PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane begins to decompose at 321.7 ℃, which is related to the addition of ATP. When ATP is roasted at about 300 ℃, the adsorbed water, zeolite water, and part of the coordinated crystal water will be removed, leading to the premature decomposition of the PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane. When calcined to 900 ℃, the residual amount of ATP is 80.1%, the residual amount of PVDF is 26.1%, and the residual amount of PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane is 32.24%. The calculation shows that the ATP content in the PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane is 11.37%, which is slightly different from the actual experimental ATP addition (15 wt%). During the preparation process of the hybrid membrane, the nano-level ATP will inevitably fall off to a certain extent, and there are also certain errors in the experiment, resulting in small test data.

Fig. 6 are respectively the surface and cross-sectional SEM images of PVDF, PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane and the PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane. From the surface maps (a)-(c), it can be seen that the surfaces of the three membranes are distributed with small holes about 100 nm in diameter and are embedded ATP in the surface of the PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane and the PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane. Compared with pure PVDF membranes, the surface roughness of PVDF/ATP and PVDF/ATP-CDs membranes have increased, which is caused by the blending of nano-level ATP in the casting solution [42]. Moreover, the grafting of nano-scale CDs on the surface of the membrane will further increase the roughness of the composite membrane. The section of the pure PVDF membrane presents a typical asymmetric structure: a dense sponge layer and a finger-like macroporous support layer [43]. With the addition of ATP, the finger hole support layer of the hybrid membrane almost disappeared, and the sponge layer increased. The addition of ATP in the casting solution has a physical cross-linking effect on the PVDF segment, which limits the movement of the polymer chain segments and causes the viscosity of the casting solution to increase [44]. This is obviously not conducive to the solvent exchange of the hybrid membrane during the membrane production process, resulting in the formation of a small pore structure (sponge layer). The enlarged structures (b2) - (c2) of the membrane sections of PVDF/ATP and PVDF/ATP-CDs show that ATP is uniformly dispersed in the membrane, and there is almost no agglomeration.

We next studied the crystal properties of the synthesized membranes through XRD analysis and the results are shown in Fig. 7(a). It can be observed that the XRD patterns of PVDF show three characteristic peaks at 2θ= 18°, 20° and 27°, indicating the α-phase (non-polar) composition of PVDF [45]. Compared with pure PVDF membrane, the XRD pattern of PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane shows a strong and sharp characteristic diffraction peak at 2θ= 8.85°, corresponding to the (110) plane of ATP [46], indicating that ATP has been successfully doped into PVDF. Inside the membrane. However, the introduction of CDs did not cause any changes or transformations in the structure of PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane, which may be caused by the low concentration of CDs in the composite membrane [47]. In addition, through the adsorption isotherms of N2 (Fig. 7(b)), the average pore diameters of PVDF, PVDF/ATP and PVDF/ATP-CDs membrane were determined to be 89.3, 88.9, and 81.2 nm, respectively ( Table 1). Compared with the pure PVDF membrane, the addition of ATP reduces the pore size of PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane, which is related to the almost disappearance of the finger pore support layer and the increase of the sponge layer in the hybrid membrane. Consistent with expectations, the grafting of CDs on the surface of the composite membrane further reduced the average pore size of the composite membrane, which may be caused by the surface-attached CDs blocking part of the surface pores.

As a commonly used material analysis technique, Raman spectroscopy was used to analyze the behavior of CDs embedded in the composite membrane matrices. As shown in Fig. 7 ©, three characteristic peaks are observed at 792 cm−1, 837 cm−1, and 877 cm−1 in the PVDF spectrum, which correspond to the CH2 swing of PVDF, the stretching of CF2 and the stretching vibration of CC, respectively [48,49]. In addition, the Raman peak at 1428 cm−1 is attributed to the shear (δ(CH2)) vibration and swing (ω (CH2)) vibration of CH2, while the peaks at 1517 cm−1 and 2978 cm−1 correspond to Bending vibration and symmetrical (vs(CH2)) vibration of CH2 [50,51]. Compared with the spectra of PVDF and PVDF/ATP, the spectra of PVDF/ATP-g-CDs show new absorption peaks at 1123 cm−1 and 1525 cm−1, which may be caused by the G band and the D band of carbon nanomaterials, suggesting the successful grafting of CDs on the surface of composite membrane.

To further explore the roughness of the membrane surface, AFM was used to test the prepared samples [52]. Fig. 8(a)-(c) show the AFM images of PVDF membrane, PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane and PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane, respectively. Consistent with the results of SEM, the surface roughness of the hybrid membrane prepared after ATP increased. The needle-shaped ATP blended in the casting solution is embedded on the surface of the hybrid membrane during the membrane formation process, which causes the surface roughness of the membrane to become larger. Similarly, the grafting of CDs further increased the surface roughness of the composite membrane [53].

The hydrophilicity of sample membranes surface was characterized by measuring their water contact angles [54], and the results are shown in Fig. 9(a). The contact angle of the pure PVDF membrane is about 100°, suggesting that the surface of the pure membrane is hydrophobic. Compared with the pure PVDF membrane, the initial contact angle of the PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane has almost no change, indicating the less influence of ATP has on the hydrophilicity of the hybrid membrane. This is because ATP binds to PVDF during the membrane formation process, which will not be destroyed by water molecules and therefore will not affect the wettability of the composite membrane surface [55]. As time goes by, the contact angle of PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane surface gradually decreases, which is related to the changes in the surface properties (roughness, pore size, etc.) of the hybrid membrane. After being grafted to the surface by CDs, the water droplets on the surface of PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane diffused rapidly within 6 s, indicating that the initial contact angle was greatly reduced. This is because the CDs on the surface of the composite membrane are super hydrophilic substances [56], which can rapidly increase the hydrophilicity of the composite membrane surface. It can be said that the grafting of CDs on the surface of the composite membrane improves the hydrophilicity of PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane.

Additionally, the pure water fluxes of pure PVDF, PVDF/ATP and PVDF/ATP-CDs membranes are measured and the results are shown in Fig. 9(b). Because of the small surface pore size and low pore density, the pure water flux of the pure PVDF membrane is only 28 L/(m2h). It is worth noting that the addition of nano-level ATP increases the water flux of the hybrid membrane by 90 L/(m2h), which may be related to the formation of surface pores [55]. After grafting with CDs, the pure water flux of PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane further increases to about 110 L/(m2h), which is almost 5 times that of pure PVDF. As a highly hydrophilic nanomaterial, CDs can not only significantly improve the hydrophilicity of the composite membrane, but also make it easy for water molecules to diffuse on the surface of the composite membrane, thereby significantly increasing the pure water flux of the composite membrane.

We next tested the adsorption properties of the original PVDF, PVDF/ATP and PVDF/ATP-CDs membranes to Cu2+, Fe3+ and Hg2+. As shown in Fig. 9(c), the pure PVDF membrane has almost no adsorption capacity for heavy metal ions. After blending with ATP, the adsorption capacity of PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane for heavy metal ions is significantly improved, which is caused by the addition of ATP with good adsorption capacity. After grafting CDs onto the PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane, the adsorption performance of PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane for Hg2+ (0.99 mg/g) continued to improve. In contrast, the composite membrane has a lower adsorption capacity for Cu2+ (0.73 mg/g) and Fe3+ (0.60 mg/g). During the grafting reaction, the abundant -NH2 and -OH on the surface of CDs occupy part of ATP, leading to a slight decrease in the amount of metal ions adsorbed by the composite membrane. Compared with Cu2+ and Fe3+, Hg2+ has a larger ionic radius, which makes it easier to coordinate with polar groups. At the same time, deformation and polarization are more likely to occur during coordination, thereby forming more stable covalent bonds [57]. As a result, the adsorption of Hg2+ by PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane is slightly higher than that of Cu2+ and Fe3+.

To verify the stability of CDs on the surface of the CDs composite membrane, the fluorescence intensity of the composite membrane was tested every 24 h [58]. As shown in Fig. 10, the fluorescence intensity of the composite membrane did not change within three days, indicating that the CDs were stably present on the surface of the composite membrane. The inset in Fig. 10 is a photo of pure PVDF, PVDF/ATP and PVDF/ATP-CDs membranes under sunlight and ultraviolet light. It can be observed that there is no obvious difference between these three types of membranes under sunlight. Under 365 nm ultraviolet irradiation, the PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane emitted bright blue fluorescence that neither pure PVDF membrane nor PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane had, which further confirmed the successful grafting of CDs on the surface of PVDF/ATP membrane.

In order to study the fluorescence quenching effect of different hybrid membranes in heavy metal ion solutions, pure PVDF membranes, PVDF/ATP hybrid membranes and PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membranes were immersed in Cu2+, Fe3+, and Hg2+ solution at a concentration of 20 mg/L. After being immersed for a certain period of time, these membranes were taken out and tested for changes in the absorption peak at 450 nm of the emission spectrum. Fig. 11 shows the fluorescence quenching spectra of pure PVDF membrane, PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane and PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane at an excitation wavelength of 380 nm. Compared with pure PVDF and PVDF/ATP hybrid membranes, PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membranes showed typical absorption peaks of CDs around 450 nm (Fig. 11), indicating that CDs have been successfully grafted onto PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane without changing the inherent fluorescence properties of CDs. After being immersed in Cu2+ or Hg2+ solution, the fluorescence intensity of PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane was found to decrease significantly, indicating that Cu2+ and Hg2+ ions have obvious fluorescence quenching effect on CDs on the surface of PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane. When in contact with Cu2+, Fe3+ and Hg2+, the -NH2 and -COOH groups of the CDs on the composite membrane coordinate with the metal ions and generate electron transfer, which causes the fluorescence of CDs to be quenched [59].

Fig. 12 briefly illustrates the adsorption and detection mechanism of heavy metal ions by the PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane. The large specific surface area of ATP can effectively increase the adsorption area of PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane for heavy metal ions [60], making the composite membrane a good natural adsorption material. In addition, the -OH groups on the surface of ATP can also form complexes with metal ions, which further enhances the adsorption of metal ions by the composite membrane. Remarkably, CDs complexes with heavy metal ions such as Cu2+ and Hg2+ through the -NH2, -COOH and other functional groups on their surface to generate electron transfer, which leads to fluorescence quenching of CDs [61]. After being grafted with CDs, the fluorescence of PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane will be quenched by heavy metal ions, so as to achieve the purpose of fluorescence detection of heavy metal ions [62].

In conclusion, by reacting CDs-KH560 with ATP in the PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane, PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane was successfully prepared with dual functions of adsorption and detection for heavy metal ions. The addition of nano-level ATP in the casting solution causes the surface roughness of the PVDF/ATP hybrid membrane to increase, which in turn induces a significant decrease in the contact angle. Due to its high hydrophilicity, the grafting of CDs further improves the wettability (contact angle drops to 0° within 6 s) and permeability (110 L/m2h) of the composite membrane, which is conducive to its application in water treatment. Moreover, owing to the high adsorption of ATP to metal ions, PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane shows a high adsorption capacity for Hg2+ (0.99 mg/g), Cu2+ (0.73 mg/g) and Fe3+ (0.60 mg/g) compared to the original PVDF membrane. After complexing with heavy metal ions, the fluorescence of CDs is quenched, which enables PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane to detect heavy metal ions. As a solid platform for heavy metal ion adsorption and detection, the PVDF/ATP-CDs composite membrane with the advantages of rapid, stable, environmentally friendly, simple and low-cost detection has a good development prospect in the field of sewage treatment.

Xuetao Tian and Dongdong Zhang conducted the experiments, analyzed the data, prepared the figures, and wrote the manuscript presented herein. Heyu Sun characterized the samples. Manman Xie and Yiping Zhao guided the research.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially sponsored by The Science and Technology Plans of Tianjin (17PTSYJC00040 and 18PTSYJC00180), Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Ministry of Education of China (No. IRT-17R80), Program for Innovative Research Team in University of Tianjin (No. TD13-5044) and Tianjin Postgraduate Research and Innovation Project (No. 2019YJSB195). We would gratefully acknowledge the help of the Analytical & Testing Center of Tiangong University.

ACS Omega

Chem. Commun.

Ind. Eng. Chem. Res.

Wang, Novel Covalently Cross-Linked Attapulgite/Poly(acrylic acid-co-acrylamide) Hybrid Hydrogels by Inverse Suspension Polymerization: Synthesis Optimization and Evaluation as Adsorbents for Toxic Heavy Metals, Industrial & Engineering Chemistry Research, 53 (2014) 4277–4285.
